name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةِ ٱلْقَدْرِ (١)
```

```english
Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment. (1)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ (٢)
```

```english
And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment? (2)
```

```arabic
لَيْلَةُ ٱلْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ (٣)
```

```english
The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months. (3)
```

```arabic
تَنَزَّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ (٤)
```

```english
In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command. (4)
```

```arabic
سَلَـٰمٌ هِىَ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ ٱلْفَجْرِ (٥)
```

```english
It is peaceful until the rising of the dawn. (5)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions the sending down of the Quran on the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) and magnifies the night by favoring it over a thousand months, and the descending of the angels and the Spirit (الروح) therein. The Surah carries the possibility of being either Meccan or Medinan, though some (1) of what has been narrated regarding the occasion of its revelation (شأن النزول) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) and others supports its being Medinan.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ** **"Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment" (97:1).** The pronoun in "We sent it down" (أنزلناه) refers to the Quran. Its apparent meaning is the entirety of the Mighty Book, not just some of its verses. This is supported by the use of the term "sending down" (الإنزال), which is apparent in considering the instantaneous descent, as opposed to "gradual revelation" (التنزيل), which is apparent in considering a step-by-step process.

In the same vein as this verse is His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ** **"By the Manifest Book! Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night" (44:2-3).** Its apparent meaning is an oath by the entirety of the Manifest Book, followed by the report of sending down that which was sworn by in its entirety.

Thus, the implication of the verses is that the Quran has a collective descent upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) other than its gradual descent which was completed over a period of twenty-three years, as indicated by His saying: **وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً** **"A Quran which We have unfolded gradually that you may recite it to mankind at intervals, and We have sent it down in stages" (17:106)**, and His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً** **"The faithless say, ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ So it is, that We may strengthen your heart with it, and We have recited it [to you] in a measured tone" (25:32).**

Therefore, no weight is given to the claim that the meaning of "We sent it down" (أنزلناه) is "We began its descent," referring to the descent of only a portion of the Quran.

__________________
(1) This refers to what indicates that the Surah was revealed after the vision of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) wherein he saw the Umayyads ascending his pulpit, which grieved him; so Allah consoled him with this Surah.

There is nothing in His speech, the Exalted, that clarifies which night this night is, other than what is in His saying, the Exalted: **شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ** **"The month of Ramadan is one in which the Quran was sent down" (2:185).** This verse, when joined with the verse of Ordainment (القدر), indicates that the night is one of the nights of the month of Ramadan. As for its specific identification beyond that, it is derived from the narrations (الأخبار), and some related matters will follow in the subsequent "Discussion of Narrations," if Allah wills.

Allah the Exalted has named it the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), and it is apparent that what is meant by *al-Qadr* (القدر) is ordainment (التقدير). Thus, it is the Night of Ordainment (ليلة التقدير) in which Allah ordains the events of the year from that night until its equivalent in the coming year—concerning life, death, sustenance, felicity, wretchedness, and other such matters. This is indicated by His saying in Surah al-Dukhan describing the night: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ** **"Every definitive matter is resolved in it, as an ordinance from Us. Indeed We have been sending [messengers] as a mercy from your Lord" (44:4-6).** The "resolving of every definitive matter" (فرق الأمر الحكيم) is nothing other than the determination of occurring events with their specificities through ordainment (التقدير).

From this, it is understood that the night recurs with the recurrence of years. Thus, in the month of Ramadan of every lunar year, there is a night in which the affairs of the year are ordained from that night until its equivalent in the following year. This is because there is no meaning in assuming a single specific night, or a limited number of nights throughout time, in which all past and future events are ordained—even if it is correct to assume that one of these recurring Nights of Ordainment was the one in which the Quran was sent down all at once.

Furthermore, His saying: **يُفْرَقُ** "is resolved"—which is a present-tense verb—is apparent in indicating continuity (الاستمرار). His saying: **خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ** "better than a thousand months" and **تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ** "the angels descend" etc., further support this.

Thus, there is no basis for the claim that it was a single specific night in which the Quran was sent down without it recurring. Likewise, the claim that it used to recur with the years during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and then Allah took it away is baseless. So too is the claim that it is one specific night throughout the entire year, or that it is within the entire year but shifts with the years—being in Ramadan one year, in Sha'ban another, and so on.

It has also been said that *al-Qadr* (القدر) means status or rank (المنزلة), and it was named the Night of Ordainment only due to the importance of its status or the status of those who worship therein. It has also been said that *al-Qadr* means constriction (الضيق), and it was named so because the earth becomes constricted by the descent of the angels. Both views, as you can see, are weak.

The sum of the verses—as you can see—is that it is a specific night in the month of Ramadan of every year, in which matters are determined according to ordainment (التقدير). This does not contradict the occurrence of change (التغير) within it regarding its actualization during the course of the year. For change in the manner of an ordained matter's actualization is one thing, and change in the ordainment itself is another—just as the possibility of change in cosmic events according to the Divine Will (المشية الإلهية) does not contradict their being fixed in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ). The Exalted said: **وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** **"and with Him is the Mother of the Book" (13:39).**

Moreover, the consolidation of matters according to their actualization has levels in terms of the presence of their causes and conditions, whether complete or incomplete. It is possible that some levels of determination occur on the Night of Ordainment, while the completion of the determination is delayed to another time. However, the narrations, as will follow, do not align with this view.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَدْراكَ ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ** **"And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment?" (97:2).** This is a metaphorical expression (كناية) for the majesty of the night's status and the greatness of its rank. This is emphasized by the explicit mention of the name time after time, where it is said: **ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ** "what is the Night of Ordainment? The Night of Ordainment is better..." rather than saying: "And what will show you what it is? It is better..."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ** **"The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months" (97:3).** This is a general explanation (بيان إجمالي) of the magnificence of the night's affair alluded to in His saying: "And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment?"

What is meant by its being "better than a thousand months" is its superiority in terms of the virtue of worship (العبادة), according to the interpretation of the commentators. This is appropriate to the purpose of the Quran and its concern with bringing people closer to Allah; thus, keeping it awake in worship is better than the worship of a thousand months. This can also be inferred from the "blessedness" mentioned in Surah al-Dukhan in His saying: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ** "Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night." There is another meaning that will follow in the subsequent "Discussion of Narrations," if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ** **"In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command" (97:4).** The word *tanazzalu* (تنزّل) is originally *tatanazzalu* (تتنزل). What is apparent regarding the Spirit (الروح) is that it is the Spirit which is from the Command (الأمر); the Exalted said: **قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي** **"Say, ‘The Spirit is of the command of my Lord’" (17:85).** Leave (الإذن) in something is the permission for it, which is the notification of the absence of any hindrance to it.

Regarding the word "from" (مِنْ) in His saying: **مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ** "with every command," it has been said to mean "with" (الباء). It has also been said to indicate the starting point of an end (ابتداء الغاية) and implies causality (السببية), meaning: "due to every divine command." It has also been said to indicate a final cause (التعليل بالغاية), meaning: "for the sake of managing every single matter." The truth is that if "the command" (الأمر) refers to the Divine Command (الأمر الإلهي) explained by His saying: **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ** **"All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is" (36:82)**, then "from" (من) indicates the starting point and implies causality. The meaning would be: the angels and the Spirit descend on the Night of Ordainment by the leave of their Lord, their descent originating from and issued by every Divine Command.

If it refers to "the matter" (الأمر) as one of the cosmic affairs and occurring events, then "from" (من) carries the meaning of the causal *lam* (اللام التعليلية), and the meaning would be: the angels and the Spirit descend on the night by the leave of their Lord for the sake of managing every cosmic matter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَلامٌ هِيَ حَتَّى مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ** **"It is peace until the rising of the dawn" (97:5).** It is stated in *al-Mufradat*: "Peace (السلام) and safety (السلامة) are the freedom from external and internal afflictions." End quote. Thus, His saying: **سَلامٌ هِيَ** "It is peace" is an allusion to the Divine Care (العناية الإلهية) in encompassing His servants who turn toward Him with mercy, and closing the door of any new affliction specific to that night. This naturally entails the weakening of the devils' plots, as alluded to in some narrations.

It has also been said: what is meant is that the angels offer salutations of peace (يسلمون) upon every believer engaged in worship whom they pass by, which reverts to the previous meaning.

The two verses—meaning His saying: **تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ** "the angels descend" until the end of the Surah—serve as an explanation (تفسير) for His saying: **لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ** "The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months."

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Shaykh al-Tusi, from Abu Dharr, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah! Is the Ordainment (القدر) something that existed during the time of the Prophets—wherein the Command (الأمر) would descend upon them—and then when they passed away, it was taken up?' He said: 'No, rather it is until the Day of Resurrection.'"

**I say:** There are several narrations with this meaning from the sources of the Sunnis.

In *al-Majma'*, from Hammad ibn 'Uthman, from Hassan ibn Abi 'Ali, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Night of Ordainment. He said: 'Seek it in the nineteenth, the twenty-first, and the twenty-third.'"

**I say:** There are others with this meaning. In some reports, the hesitation is between two nights: the twenty-first and the twenty-third, such as the narration of al-'Ayyashi from 'Abd al-Wahid from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him). It is understood from the narrations that it is the night of the twenty-third, and it was only left unspecified to magnify its affair so that it would not be taken lightly by committing sins.

In the same source, also in a narration of 'Abdullah ibn Bukayr from Zurarah from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (peace be upon them), who said: "The night of the twenty-third is the Night of al-Juhani." Its story is that he said to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "My home is far from Medina, so command me with a night in which I may enter [the city]." So he commanded him with the night of the twenty-third.

**I say:** The tradition of al-Juhani—whose name was 'Abdullah ibn Unays al-Ansari—is also narrated through Sunni channels; it was cited in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Malik and al-Bayhaqi.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Zurarah, who said: "Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'The ordainment (التقدير) is on the nineteenth, the consolidation (الإبرام) is on the night of the twenty-first, and the final execution (الإمضاء) is on the night of the twenty-third.'"

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning.

The reports of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) are in agreement that it remains and recurs every year, that it is one of the nights of the month of Ramadan, and that it is one of the three nights.

As for the Sunni sources, the narrations differ with a strange variation that can hardly be cataloged. What is well-known among them is that it is the twenty-seventh night, in which the Quran was sent down. Whoever wishes to find them should refer to *al-Durr al-Manthur* and other compendiums.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Khatib extracted from Ibn al-Musayyib, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I saw the Umayyads ascending my pulpit, and that distressed me.' So Allah sent down: 'Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment.'"

**I say:** Something similar was also narrated by al-Khatib in his *Tarikh* from Ibn 'Abbas, and also what carries this meaning from al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi from al-Hasan ibn 'Ali. There are many narrations with this meaning through Shi'i channels from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), stating that Allah the Exalted consoled His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by granting the Night of Ordainment and making it better than a thousand months, which is the duration of the Umayyad rule.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Abi 'Umayr from more than one person from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), that one of our companions—and I only know him to be Sa'id al-Samman—said to him: "How can the Night of Ordainment be better than a thousand months?" He said: "Action (العمل) therein is better than action in a thousand months that do not contain the Night of Ordainment."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from al-Fudayl, Zurarah, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Humran, that he asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ** "Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night." He said: "Yes, it is the Night of Ordainment, and it is in every year in the month of Ramadan—in the last ten days. The Quran was not sent down except on the Night of Ordainment. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ** 'Every definitive matter is resolved in it.'"

He said: "Everything that will be in that year until its equivalent in the coming year is ordained on the Night of Ordainment: good and evil, obedience and disobedience, births, appointed terms (أجل), and sustenance. So whatever is ordained in that night and decreed is of the finalized matters (المحتوم)—yet for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, there is Will (المشية) therein."

He said: "I said: **لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ** 'The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months'—what did He mean by that?" He replied: "Righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) therein—of prayer, zakat, and various types of good—are better than action in a thousand months that do not contain the Night of Ordainment. Were it not for what Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, multiplies for the believers, they would not reach [the goal]; but Allah multiplies the good deeds for them."

**I say:** His saying, "and for Allah there is Will therein," refers to the absolute nature of His power, the Exalted. He has the right to will whatever He wills, even if He has finalized it. For His making a matter necessary does not negate His absolute power; He has the right to revoke the finalized decree (القضاء المحتوم), even if He never wills to do so.

In *al-Majma'*, Ibn 'Abbas narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: "When it is the Night of Ordainment, the angels—who are the inhabitants of the Lote Tree of the Farthest Boundary (سدرة المنتهى), among whom is Gabriel—descend. Gabriel descends with banners; he plants one banner on my grave, one banner on the Holy House (بيت المقدس), one banner in the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام), and one banner on Mount Sinai. He does not leave a single believing man or woman therein except that he offers them salutations of peace—except for the habitual wine-drinker, the eater of swine flesh (1), and the one who smears himself with saffron."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Sa'd ibn 'Abdullah, with his chain of transmission from Abu Basir, who said: "I was with Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) and he mentioned something regarding the Imam when he is born. He said: 'He becomes entitled to an increase of the Spirit (الروح) on the Night of Ordainment.' I said: 'May I be your ransom! Is the Spirit not Gabriel?' He said: 'Gabriel is of the angels, and the Spirit is greater than the angels. Does Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, not say: **تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ** "The angels and the Spirit descend"?'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding the Night of Ordainment and its virtue are very numerous. In some of them, signs are mentioned for it that are neither permanent nor frequent—such as the sun rising on its morning without rays, or the air being temperate therein—which we have overlooked.
